name: Montes del Cerrato Loop
name_original: Hornillos de Cerrato, Tabanera de Cerrato y Antigüedad – circular desde Baltanás
description: This moderate 28.9-mile touring cycling route from Baltanás explores the El Cerrato region, passing historic sites and diverse landscapes.
region_intro: Touring cycling around El Cerrato features a landscape of rolling hills and plains, with elevations generally ranging from 700 to 900 meters above sea level. The region is characterized by limestone páramos separated by valleys, providing varied terrain for cyclists. Along rivers like the Pisuerga and Arlanza, forests of poplar, willow, and juniper offer shaded sections. This topography allows for engaging routes without extreme mountainous challenges. Best touring cycling routes around El Cerrato The most popular touring cycling route is Hontoria de Cerrato, Valle de Cerrato y Soto de Cerrato – circular desde Venta de Baños, a 22.4 miles (36.0 km) trail that takes 2 hours 23 minutes to complete. This moderate route navigates through valleys and offers views of the region's characteristic rolling hills. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Montes del Cerrato Loop, a moderate 28.9 miles (46.5 km) path. This route explores the "Montes del Cerrato," providing a mix of terrain and scenic vistas. Local touring cyclists also love the Canal de Castilla Loop from Dueñas, a 26.8 miles (43.2 km) trail leading through the historic Canal de Castilla, often completed in about 3 hours 28 minutes. Touring cycling around El Cerrato is defined by rolling hills, limestone páramos, and riverine forests. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to various ability levels. The routes in El Cerrato are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 25 reviews. More than 380 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore El Cerrato's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in El Cerrato?
A: El Cerrato offers a good selection of touring cycling routes, with over 30 options available. These routes cater to various skill levels, ensuring there's something for everyone.

Q: What is the general difficulty level of touring cycling routes in El Cerrato?
A: The majority of touring cycling routes in El Cerrato are classified as moderate, with 26 such routes. There are also 3 easy routes perfect for beginners or a relaxed ride, and 4 more challenging routes for experienced cyclists seeking a greater adventure.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about the routes in El Cerrato?
A: The komoot community highly rates the touring cycling experience in El Cerrato, with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 25 reviews. Cyclists often praise the region's characteristic rolling hills, varied terrain, and the scenic beauty of the limestone páramos and riverine forests.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners in El Cerrato?
A: Yes, El Cerrato offers several easy touring cycling routes. One excellent option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14938426" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lock 33, Canal de Castilla loop from Villamuriel de Cerrato&lt;/a&gt;, which is 13.2 miles (21.3 km) long and takes approximately 2 hours to complete, offering a gentle introduction to the region's landscapes.

Q: Can I find circular touring cycling routes in El Cerrato?
A: Absolutely. Many routes in El Cerrato are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1175544553/montes-del-cerrato-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Montes del Cerrato Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a popular moderate route covering 28.9 miles (46.5 km), offering a comprehensive tour of the region's varied terrain.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural sights to explore along the cycling routes in El Cerrato?
A: El Cerrato is rich in history and culture. Along your rides, you can discover significant landmarks such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3865822" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lock 33, Canal de Castilla&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4742473" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Soto Albúrez Lock&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4885216" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Santa María la Mayor&lt;/a&gt;. The region also boasts the ancient San Juan de Baños Basilica and the Roman Bridge on the Arlanza river in Palenzuela, offering fascinating stops for cultural enrichment.

Q: What kind of natural features can I expect to see while cycling in El Cerrato?
A: El Cerrato's landscape is characterized by rolling hills and limestone páramos, creating a unique 'maze of valleys and natural nooks.' You'll also find forests of poplar, willow, and juniper along rivers like the Pisuerga, providing shaded sections and diverse scenery. The region is part of the Natura 2000 network, highlighting its ecological significance.

Q: Are there any routes that follow the Canal de Castilla?
A: Yes, the historic Canal de Castilla offers picturesque and relatively flat sections ideal for touring cycling. A great option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1143789912/canal-de-castilla-loop-from-duenas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canal de Castilla Loop from Dueñas&lt;/a&gt;, a 26.8 miles (43.2 km) trail that takes you through this iconic waterway, often completed in about 3 hours 28 minutes.

Q: What are some notable towns or villages to visit while cycling in El Cerrato?
A: El Cerrato is dotted with charming villages and towns. Dueñas is an historically important town and a good starting point for many routes. Baltanás, the largest municipality, features the impressive San Millán church and a unique 'Barrio de Bodegas' (wine cellars district). Astudillo also offers historical interest with its Santa Eugenia church.

Q: Is it possible to combine cycling with local gastronomy and wine experiences in El Cerrato?
A: Absolutely. El Cerrato is gaining recognition for combining cycling with enology, culture, and heritage. You can explore ancient wineries and traditional shepherd's huts, and savor local produce like Palenzuela Onion and Torquemada Pepper, adding a delicious cultural dimension to your cycling journey.

Q: Are there any longer, more challenging touring cycling routes in El Cerrato?
A: For those seeking a longer and more challenging ride, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38162211" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente Mayor de Palencia – Calahorra de Ribas Locks loop from Venta de Baños&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult route spanning 48.8 miles (78.6 km). This extensive tour provides a significant workout and a deep exploration of the region's diverse landscapes.

Q: What kind of wildlife can I spot while cycling in El Cerrato?
A: El Cerrato is home to diverse fauna, especially in its protected areas. Along river channels, you might spot herons, otters, and even the Pyrenean desman. The moors are home to hares, rabbits, partridges, and turtle doves. The region is also known for its ornithological richness, with opportunities to observe various bird species, including the Great Bustard and Little Bustard.

Q: Are there any routes that offer views of the wider landscape?
A: Yes, many routes provide expansive views of the region's characteristic rolling hills and plains. For a specific viewpoint, consider routes that pass near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3464062" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Fields of Tierra de Campos&lt;/a&gt;, offering panoramic vistas of the surrounding agricultural landscapes.
tips: Baltanás, located in the Cerrato Palentino, was the old historical capital of the Cerrato Castellano and the Cerrato Palentino. In the urban area is the church of San Millán, the Palace-Hospital of Santo Tomás and the Convent of San Francisco. Baltanás also has excavated cellars declared an Asset of Cultural Interest.

Built in the 16th century on a previous building in the 9th century, the castle stands on a hill that dominates the surrounding landscape. The fortification still preserves part of its walls, towers and defensive elements. It was the scene of important historical events, such as the Enríquez revolt and the visit of Juana la Loca.

Town in the Cerrato region. It stands out for its Parish Church of the Assumption in Baroque style and the F-4 Phantom II combat plane in homage to the Martín Campos brothers, pilots of the Spanish military aviation. You can also visit the hermitage of Villella, the shepherd's huts, the beehives and various historical sources.

faqs.question: Where does the Hornillos de Cerrato, Tabanera de Cerrato y Antigüedad circular route start, and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The route typically starts in Baltanás. You'll find parking options in Baltanás, which is known for its Barrio de Bodegas (winery district) and the Cerrato Museum. It's a good place to begin your cycling adventure.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route offers a "landscape of contrasts" through the El Cerrato region. You'll encounter a mix of plains, unique hills, and elevations. While much of the route is likely on paved surfaces, some sections may be unpaved or challenging for touring bicycles, requiring a good fitness level.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: The route is classified as a "moderate bicycle route" and is designed for touring cyclists with good physical fitness. It covers approximately 46.5 kilometers with about 450 meters of ascent, so it might be challenging for absolute beginners.

faqs.question: What can I see along the Hornillos de Cerrato, Tabanera de Cerrato y Antigüedad route?
faqs.answer: You'll pass through diverse scenery including Mediterranean forests, pine forests, oak groves, and cultivated fields. Key points of interest include the Baltanás Wineries, the historical Hornillos de Cerrato Castle, and the Antigüedad Town Center and F-4 Phantom II Memorial. You can also spot local wildlife like deer, foxes, and various steppe birds.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The scenery along the route changes color depending on the season, offering different experiences throughout the year. The El Cerrato region is generally pleasant for cycling, but consider spring and autumn for milder temperatures and vibrant landscapes. Summer can be hot, and winter might bring colder conditions.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes, pubs, or accommodation options along the route?
faqs.answer: The starting point, Baltanás, is known for its gastronomy and wine tourism, offering options for food and drink. Hornillos de Cerrato and Antigüedad are smaller towns, but you may find local establishments there. It's advisable to check availability beforehand, especially in smaller villages.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route itself is a public path, specific regulations for dogs on cycling routes can vary. Generally, dogs are allowed on leash on public roads and trails. However, it's always best to ensure your dog is well-behaved around bicycles and other people, and to carry water for them, especially on longer rides.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to cycle this route in the El Cerrato region?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to access and cycle this route. The El Cerrato region is not a designated national park with entry fees, and the route uses public access roads and trails.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with any other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through small sections of other paths, including Calle Ortigas and Calle Santa Columba.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife can I expect to see along the route?
faqs.answer: The diverse landscape of El Cerrato provides opportunities to observe local wildlife. You might spot deer, foxes, and various steppe birds, including impressive griffin vultures and golden eagles, especially in the more natural sections of the route.

faqs.question: Is this a circular route?
faqs.answer: Yes, this is a circular route, meaning it starts and ends in the same location, Baltanás, allowing you to return to your starting point without needing additional transport.

waypoints.name: Antigüedad Town Center and F-4 Phantom II Memorial
